title: Settlement Signature
entries: The whole place is built around one enormous old tree, its roots buckling the market square.
A crooked tower that has been a little out of true, and a little proud of it, for as long as anyone remembers.
One paved street runs through it; the rest are mud, and the paved one is fiercely proud of itself.
Every door is painted the same shade of blue, by a custom no one can quite explain.
A stone bridge far grander than the place deserves, left by someone long gone.
The bells ring at odd hours, to a schedule only the old ringer understands.
A weekly market draws people from a surprising distance for one particular good.
The oldest building is a shrine no one worships at any more, kept spotless all the same.
A local dish so pungent that travellers smell the town before they see it.
The streets are unusually clean; the townsfolk are quietly, competitively houseproud.
A weathered statue in the square whose subject everyone has forgotten but no one will move.
Cats. Far too many cats, all of them plainly in charge.
A great waterwheel groans day and night; the sound is so constant the locals no longer hear it.
The town keeps its clocks a quarter-hour fast, and swears the rest of the world is late.
A single, improbably famous craftsman works here, and the town lives half in their shadow.
Washing is strung across every lane overhead, so the streets are roofed in drying linen.
An annual festival the town takes far too seriously, involving a goose and a great deal of shouting.
The well in the square is said to grant a wish; it mostly grants cold, clean water.
Terraced gardens climb every spare wall — the whole town is quietly, ferociously in bloom.
One family has held the same feud with another for six generations; no one remembers why.
A crumbling town wall now used mostly for drying fish and courting couples.
The inn is older than the town and insists, loudly, on being addressed as such.
Everything smells faintly of woodsmoke and someone's very good bread.
A row of ancient standing stones the town has simply built around and named after taverns.
The locals speak with an accent so thick that neighbouring villages need a moment to follow.
A painted line on the road marks where a saint once stopped to rest; carts still swerve to avoid it.
The market never closes — there is always someone, at any hour, selling something to someone.
Half the roofs are turf and half are slate, in two stubborn, opposed halves of town.
A tame raven has the run of the place and is widely believed to understand more than it lets on.
The town is proud of a battle it once lost, and tells the story better every year.
Bridges everywhere — the place is stitched together over a dozen little channels and streams.
A single lamp is kept burning in the tallest window, for a ship or a person long overdue.
The children run a strict, unwritten set of rules for the alleys that no adult fully understands.
A hot spring feeds the public baths; the whole town is cleaner and slower for it.
The chapel bell is cracked and sounds terrible, and the town would riot before it was replaced.
Vines have swallowed the old gatehouse whole; in summer it is simply a green mound.
The town square doubles as a threshing floor at harvest and a dance floor after.
A famous view — everyone who passes through is marched to the one spot to admire it.
The place brews something local and potent that it is quietly, dangerously proud of.
Every house keeps a small carved figure by the door, and no two are alike.
The main street bends hard around a boulder no one has ever managed to move.
A covered market hall on old timber posts, dark and cool and smelling of centuries of trade.
The town runs on a bartered favour economy as much as coin, and outsiders find it baffling.
One long-dead hero is buried under the crossroads, and travellers still leave them a coin.
A colony of storks nests on the chimneys; the town plans its year around their coming and going.
The place floods a little every spring and simply lives above the waterline for a month.
Its walls are hung with faded guild banners for trades that no longer exist here.
A stubborn old ferryman refuses to be replaced by the perfectly good bridge upstream.
The town is built on a slope so steep that its two ends barely speak to each other.
